primary_diagnosis.name: Vasovagal (reflex) syncope with minor closed head injury

comorbidities.name: Minor closed head injury (occipital contusion) secondary to the fall

pathophysiology_summary: Reflex (vasovagal) syncope: prolonged standing in a warm environment plus mild volume depletion promotes venous pooling and reduced cardiac preload. A paradoxical autonomic reflex (the Bezold-Jarisch response) then produces vasodilatation and bradycardia, transiently dropping cerebral perfusion and causing a brief self-terminating loss of consciousness with loss of postural tone. Recovery is rapid and complete once the patient is horizontal and cerebral perfusion is restored. The classic prodrome (visual greying, lightheadedness), bystander-observed pallor, and absence of a postictal state distinguish it from seizure; the vagal prodrome after prolonged standing and the absence of exertion, chest pain or palpitations argue against an arrhythmic cardiac cause. The head strike during the uncontrolled fall introduces a separate, independent risk of intracranial injury that must be assessed on its own merits.

differential.evidence_basis: Transient loss of consciousness with rapid full recovery in a young adult after prolonged standing.
Any first syncope may be the only warning of a lethal arrhythmia or structural heart disease; cannot be excluded without an ECG.
Transient loss of consciousness with a fall could be a seizure until postictal features and tongue biting are screened out.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatic intracranial injury from the head strike
differential.evidence_basis: Witnessed head strike with brief loss of consciousness — an evolving intracranial bleed is the separate time-critical concern.
Classic vagal prodrome, prolonged warm standing, bystander pallor, rapid recovery, no exertion, no cardiac symptoms.
Dehydration is present, but the event occurred after prolonged standing rather than immediately on rising — less typical.
Classic prodrome, pallor, rapid recovery, no tongue biting, no incontinence, no postictal confusion.
Made less likely by the vagal prodrome and negative family history, but not excludable remotely — needs a 12-lead ECG.

differential.diagnosis: Reflex (vasovagal) syncope with minor head injury
differential.evidence_basis: Clinical picture is classic vasovagal; final confirmation requires in-person cardiac (ECG) and neurological assessment plus head-injury observation, which telehealth cannot supply.
Retained on the list until an ECG is normal — the honest telehealth position is 'benign pattern, not yet excluded'.

red_flags.description: Head strike with transient loss of consciousness — potential for an evolving intracranial bleed
red_flags.clinical_significance: A minor head injury can be followed hours later by an expanding extradural/subdural haematoma; worsening headache, vomiting, drowsiness or focal signs mandate emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed intracranial bleed can progress to coma and death overnight if the patient is simply told to rest at home without safety-netting.

red_flags.description: First unexplained transient loss of consciousness — possible cardiac (arrhythmic) syncope not excludable without an ECG
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cardiac syncope can be the only warning of a life-threatening arrhythmia or structural heart disease; a normal 12-lead ECG is a minimum step that telehealth cannot perform.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Falsely labelling a cardiac faint as a simple reflex faint can precede sudden cardiac death.

red_flags.description: Absence of postictal state, tongue biting and incontinence — pertinent negatives that argue against seizure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence supports a reflex faint over a seizure, but does not remove the need for in-person assessment of the first collapse.

red_flags.description: Exertional syncope, palpitations or a family history of sudden death (features of dangerous cardiac syncope)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence lowers the probability of a malignant cause but is reassurance, not exclusion; an ECG is still required.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the benign vasovagal story and clearing the patient remotely without arranging the ECG and in-person assessment that would exclude a cardiac cause.
Focusing on the cause of the faint and forgetting that the head strike with loss of consciousness is a separate, independently escalatable risk needing observation and red-flag advice.
Calling the transient loss of consciousness a seizure and starting a neurology pathway, missing the classic reflex-syncope prodrome and pallor.

Reproducing the in-person clinician's disposition (reassure and send home) even though the neuro exam, cardiac auscultation, orthostatic vitals and skull palpation that justified it cannot be done over chat.

risk_scores.tool_name: Canadian Syncope Risk Score (conceptual — requires in-person ECG and vitals)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Cannot be computed via telehealth (needs ECG, troponin and measured vitals); clinical pattern is low-risk but unconfirmed
risk_scores.clinical_implication: The score itself demonstrates the telehealth limit — a formal risk stratification depends on data only obtainable in person.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG to exclude an arrhythmic or structural cardiac cause
Cardiovascular auscultation for murmurs of structural heart disease
Full neurological examination (cranial nerves, power, coordination, pupils)
Palpation of the skull for a fracture or bony step-off
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (emergency department or urgent care) for a 12-lead ECG, neurological examination and head-injury observation; call 000 immediately if any head-injury red flag (worsening headache, repeated vomiting, drowsiness, confusion, weakness, seizure) or any recurrent collapse, chest pain or palpitations develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The diagnosis is very likely benign, but the danger here is UNDER-action: reassuring the patient as a 'simple faint' remotely would miss a cardiac arrhythmia that needs an ECG and an evolving intracranial bleed that needs observation.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of syncope
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A 12-lead ECG is recommended in the initial evaluation of all patients with syncope; features of the prodrome and triggers help distinguish reflex from cardiac syncope.
Any loss of consciousness following a head injury is an indication for medical assessment and structured observation for deterioration.
